Name: curvature_aware_precision_routing
Title: Training-Free Curvature-Aware Precision Routing: Spectral Diagnostics for Deterministic Mixed-Precision Inference
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that layer-wise precision requirements can be deterministically inferred from pre-execution spectral diagnostics of activation covariance and gradient flow geometry, without training or calibration data. Specifically, we propose that the Hessian conditioning number and singular value decay rate of intermediate representations directly encode numerical stability requirements, enabling a training-free routing protocol that couples geometric subspace analysis with provable PAC-Bayes generalization bounds. This setting is essential because existing mixed-precision strategies rely on post-hoc calibration or heuristic sensitivity metrics, obscuring the mechanistic relationship between representation geometry and precision requirements. Our approach directly addresses the gap by operationalizing training-free diagnostics into deterministic, theoretically-grounded routing decisions with explicit numerical stability guarantees.
Related Work: Existing mixed-precision inference strategies fall into three categories: (1) post-training quantization (PTQ) methods that require calibration data and iterative sensitivity analysis (e.g., BRECQ, OCS), (2) learned quantization schemes requiring fine-tuning, and (3) heuristic layer-wise precision assignment based on weight magnitudes or activation ranges. Recent work on mechanistic interpretability (e.g., singular value analysis of attention heads) and representation geometry has demonstrated that spectral properties encode meaningful algorithmic structure, but these analyses remain primarily descriptive rather than prescriptive for optimization. Concurrent work on condition number estimation (e.g., Hutchinson trace estimators) provides tools for spectral diagnostics but lacks integration with precision routing. Our proposal is novel because it: (i) establishes a formal, training-free diagnostic loop that maps spectral geometry directly to precision requirements via explicit condition number thresholds, (ii) grounds precision routing in PAC-Bayes generalization theory rather than empirical sensitivity, and (iii) operationalizes curvature-aware routing with strict computational overhead bounds suitable for single-GPU validation. This differs fundamentally from prior work by replacing calibration-dependent sensitivity metrics with deterministic, pre-execution geometric analysis.
Abstract: Mixed-precision inference offers substantial computational savings but requires principled layer-wise precision assignment. Existing methods rely on post-hoc calibration or heuristic sensitivity analysis, obscuring the mechanistic relationship between representation geometry and numerical stability. We propose a training-free curvature-aware precision routing framework that infers layer-wise precision requirements directly from pre-execution spectral diagnostics. Our key insight is that the Hessian conditioning number and singular value decay rate of intermediate activations encode quantization-induced perturbation amplification, enabling deterministic precision routing without calibration. We develop a diagnostic protocol that: (1) estimates layer-wise condition numbers via spectral methods (Hutchinson trace, power iteration), (2) computes stability radii under precision switching using explicit perturbation bounds, and (3) routes precision assignments via a deterministic threshold-based algorithm grounded in PAC-Bayes generalization theory. We formalize numerical stability guarantees through explicit loss-perturbation bounds and condition number constraints, ensuring all routing decisions satisfy provable generalization bounds. Empirical validation on ImageNet and language model inference demonstrates that our training-free routing achieves comparable accuracy to calibration-based methods while reducing calibration overhead by 10-100×. Ablations isolate the contribution of spectral diagnostics, validate threshold selection via cross-architecture consistency, and quantify amortized overhead (< 5% inference cost). Our framework establishes a rigorous pipeline for mechanistic precision routing, bridging representation geometry with deployment-grade inference optimization.

Experiments.name: Spectral Diagnostic Validation
Experiments.description: For each layer in ResNet-50 and DistilBERT, compute: (i) layer-wise condition number κ via Hutchinson trace estimator (50 random probes), (ii) singular value decay exponent α from top-k SVD (k=min(100, layer_dim)), (iii) gradient covariance decay via power iteration on activation covariance matrix. Validate that these spectral estimates remain stable across 5 random seeds and different batch compositions (batch size 32, 128, 256). Establish baseline: condition numbers correlate with layer sensitivity to quantization (measured via per-layer KL divergence under INT8 quantization). Metric: Spearman correlation between κ and quantization sensitivity > 0.7.
Experiments.algorithmic_detail: For condition number estimation: κ̂ = ||Av̂||/||v̂|| where v̂ is the top eigenvector of A^T A (A = layer activation matrix). Use power iteration with 20 iterations, random initialization. For singular value decay: fit log(σ_i) = -α·i + c via least squares on top-100 singular values. Compute via randomized SVD (Halko et al.) with target rank 100, oversampling 10.
Experiments.evaluation_metrics: Spectral stability (coefficient of variation < 0.1 across seeds), correlation with empirical quantization sensitivity, computational overhead (< 2% of layer inference time)

Experiments.name: Precision Routing via Condition Number Thresholding
Experiments.description: Develop a deterministic routing algorithm: For each layer, compute κ_i and assign precision as: if κ_i < τ_1, use INT8; if τ_1 ≤ κ_i < τ_2, use INT16; else use FP32. Thresholds τ_1, τ_2 are derived from PAC-Bayes bounds (see theory below). Test on ResNet-50, DistilBERT, OPT-1.3B. Compare against: (1) baseline uniform INT8, (2) BRECQ calibration-based routing, (3) magnitude-based heuristic routing. Metric: Top-1 accuracy on ImageNet (ResNet) and perplexity on WikiText-103 (language models).
Experiments.algorithmic_detail: PAC-Bayes threshold derivation: For a layer with loss perturbation bound Δℓ_i = κ_i · ε_q (where ε_q is quantization noise), the generalization bound is ℓ(h) ≤ ℓ̂(h) + √(κ_i · ε_q / m) + O(1/√m). Set τ_1 such that κ_1 · ε_INT8 ≤ δ_1 (target perturbation budget), and τ_2 such that κ_2 · ε_INT16 ≤ δ_2. Allocate perturbation budget δ = Σ_i δ_i such that total loss increase ≤ 0.5% (empirically calibrated). Routing is deterministic: no calibration loop.
Experiments.evaluation_metrics: Accuracy drop vs. FP32 baseline, memory reduction (GB), latency speedup, comparison with BRECQ (calibration time, accuracy parity)

Experiments.name: Perturbation Stability Analysis and Generalization Bounds
Experiments.description: Formalize loss perturbation under precision switching. For layer i with weight matrix W_i, activation A_i, compute: (i) quantization noise bound ε_i = max_x ||Q(x) - x|| where Q is quantizer, (ii) layer-wise loss perturbation Δℓ_i = ||∂ℓ/∂z_i|| · κ_i · ε_i (z_i is layer input), (iii) cumulative perturbation bound via chain rule. Verify that empirical loss increase matches theoretical bound to within 20% across 10 random seeds. Derive explicit PAC-Bayes bound: with probability 1-δ, ℓ_test(h) ≤ ℓ_train(h) + Σ_i Δℓ_i + O(√(log(1/δ)/m)).
Experiments.algorithmic_detail: Quantization noise: ε_INT8 = (max(W) - min(W)) / 255 (uniform quantization). Gradient norm via backprop: ||∂ℓ/∂z_i|| estimated via Hutchinson trace on Hessian of loss w.r.t. layer input. Cumulative bound: Δℓ_total = Σ_i κ_i · ε_i · ||∂ℓ/∂z_i||. Empirical loss increase: measure on validation set by comparing FP32 vs. mixed-precision predictions.
Experiments.evaluation_metrics: Ratio of empirical loss increase to theoretical bound (target: 0.8-1.2), tightness of PAC-Bayes bound (gap between train and test loss), coverage of confidence intervals across 10 seeds

Experiments.name: Cross-Architecture Generalization of Thresholds
Experiments.description: Test whether thresholds τ_1, τ_2 derived on ResNet-50 generalize to ResNet-101, ResNet-18, and EfficientNet-B0. Similarly, test DistilBERT thresholds on BERT-base and RoBERTa-base. Hypothesis: thresholds should be architecture-invariant because they encode fundamental numerical stability properties. Metric: accuracy drop consistency (< 0.5% variance across architectures).
Experiments.algorithmic_detail: Derive thresholds on one architecture (ResNet-50) using perturbation budget allocation. Apply same τ_1, τ_2 to other architectures without retraining. Measure top-1 accuracy drop on ImageNet for each architecture.
Experiments.evaluation_metrics: Accuracy drop across architectures (std < 0.5%), threshold consistency (κ distributions across architectures have similar quantiles)

Experiments.name: Ablation: Spectral Diagnostic Components
Experiments.description: Isolate contributions of: (i) condition number alone, (ii) singular value decay rate alone, (iii) gradient covariance decay alone, (iv) all three combined. For each variant, measure accuracy drop vs. FP32 baseline on ResNet-50 and DistilBERT. Hypothesis: condition number is primary driver; singular value decay and gradient decay provide secondary refinement.
Experiments.algorithmic_detail: Variant 1 (κ only): Route based on κ_i alone. Variant 2 (SVD decay only): Route based on α_i (exponent). Variant 3 (gradient decay only): Route based on spectral decay of gradient covariance. Variant 4: Route based on weighted combination (κ_i + 0.3·α_i + 0.2·grad_decay_i).
Experiments.evaluation_metrics: Accuracy drop for each variant, relative contribution (via regression analysis), computational overhead per diagnostic

Experiments.name: Overhead Quantification and Amortization
Experiments.description: Measure end-to-end overhead of diagnostic loop: (i) time to compute spectral estimates (Hutchinson, power iteration), (ii) time to route precision assignments, (iii) time for mixed-precision inference. Target: diagnostic overhead < 5% of total inference cost. Test on single GPU (A100, V100) with batch sizes 32, 128, 256.
Experiments.algorithmic_detail: Diagnostic loop: (1) Hutchinson trace (50 probes): O(layer_dim × 50), (2) power iteration (20 iterations): O(layer_dim × 20), (3) routing (threshold comparison): O(num_layers). Amortize over multiple inferences: compute diagnostics once, reuse for 100+ inferences.
Experiments.evaluation_metrics: Wall-clock time (ms) for diagnostics, fraction of total inference time, amortized cost per inference

Experiments.name: Comparison with Calibration-Based Baselines
Experiments.description: Direct comparison with BRECQ, OCS, and magnitude-based heuristics. For each method: (i) measure calibration time (if applicable), (ii) measure accuracy on ImageNet/WikiText-103, (iii) measure latency and memory. Our method has zero calibration time. Metric: accuracy parity with < 0.5% drop, 10-100× reduction in calibration overhead.
Experiments.algorithmic_detail: BRECQ: use official implementation, calibration on 32 images per class. OCS: calibration on 1000 random images. Magnitude heuristic: assign precision based on ||W||_∞ percentiles. Our method: apply spectral routing with no calibration data.
Experiments.evaluation_metrics: Accuracy, calibration time, latency, memory, Pareto frontier (accuracy vs. calibration time)

Risk Factors and Limitations.risk: Spectral Estimates May Be Noisy on Small Layers
Risk Factors and Limitations.mitigation: For layers with dim < 64, fall back to magnitude-based heuristics or default to FP32. Quantify layer sizes where spectral estimates become unstable (< 50 samples for condition number estimation).

Risk Factors and Limitations.risk: Condition Number May Not Fully Capture Quantization Sensitivity
Risk Factors and Limitations.mitigation: Validate empirically that κ correlates with quantization sensitivity (target: Spearman > 0.7). If correlation is weak, incorporate additional diagnostics (e.g., Lipschitz constants, activation range statistics).

Risk Factors and Limitations.risk: PAC-Bayes Bounds May Be Loose in Practice
Risk Factors and Limitations.mitigation: Empirically validate that actual loss increase is within 1.5× theoretical bound. If bounds are too loose, tighten via localized bounds or empirical calibration of constants.

Risk Factors and Limitations.risk: Thresholds May Not Generalize Across Domains
Risk Factors and Limitations.mitigation: Test on multiple domains (vision, NLP, speech). If thresholds diverge, derive domain-specific adjustments or incorporate domain-aware diagnostics.

Risk Factors and Limitations.risk: Computational Overhead of Spectral Methods May Exceed 5% Target
Risk Factors and Limitations.mitigation: Use randomized algorithms (Halko et al.) and limit number of probes. Amortize diagnostics over multiple inferences. If overhead exceeds 5%, reduce probe counts or use approximate estimators.

Risk Factors and Limitations.risk: Routing May Conflict with Hardware Constraints
Risk Factors and Limitations.mitigation: Ensure precision assignments align with hardware support (e.g., INT8 cores on GPUs). If conflicts arise, adjust routing to respect hardware capabilities.
Current framework assigns fixed precision per layer. Dynamic routing per-token would require additional overhead; defer to future work.

Risk Factors and Limitations.risk: Evaluation Limited to Inference; Training Dynamics Unclear
Risk Factors and Limitations.mitigation: Scope is inference-only. Training-time precision assignment (e.g., quantization-aware training) is orthogonal; establish clear boundary.
